1: אֵזוֹר פִּשְׁתִּים (ezor pishtim, linen belt/waistcloth): the sign-act (mashal) begins with an intimate garment — worn next to the skin, signifying the closeness of the YHWH-Israel bond. Linen (pishtim) was the material of priestly garments (Ex Jeremiah 28:39), adding priestly overtones to the sign. אַל-תְּבִיאֵהוּ בַמָּיִם (al-tevi'ehu vamayim, do not put it in water): kept unwashed, representing the uncleansed intimacy of the relationship as it was meant to be.
2: Jeremiah buys the sash and wears it according to YHWH's word — sign-acts require faithful literal enactment; the prophet's body becomes the medium of the prophetic message.
3: YHWH's second word: go to the Euphrates (Perat) and hide it there in a crevice of rock. The Euphrates is Babylon — the hiding place is the location of exile.
4: The command is enacted — whether by literal travel or visionary enactment (the distance makes literal two-way travel possible but logistically demanding). The sign-act is narrated to be read, not audited for logistics.
5: Jeremiah went and hid the sash at the Euphrates as commanded — the prophet's obedience in sign-acts is as constitutive of the message as the spoken word. The enacted word is YHWH's word.
6: Many days later YHWH says: arise, retrieve the sash — the time gap represents the exile period. What was hidden is now to be recovered and examined as evidence.
7: וְהִנֵּה נִשְׁחַת הָאֵזוֹר (vehinneh nishchat ha'ezor, and behold the sash was ruined): shachat (ruin/corruption) — the same root used for the moral corruption of the people. לֹא יִצְלָח לַכֹּל (lo yitzlach lakol, good for nothing at all): completely useless. The ruined garment is the sermon before the sermon.
8: The word of YHWH came again to interpret the sign — the sign-act requires prophetic interpretation. Ezekiel's sign-acts follow the same pattern; Jesus's parables function similarly.
9: כָּכָה אַשְׁחִית (kakha ashchit, in this way I will ruin): the sign is the sermon. גְּאוֹן יְהוּדָה (ge'on Yehudah) and גְּאוֹן יְרוּשָׁלַם (ge'on Yerushalayim, the great pride of Judah and Jerusalem): their pride will be ruined precisely as the sash was — the pride-judgment link runs throughout the prophets.
10: הָעָם הָרַע הַזֶּה (ha'am hara' hazeh, this evil people): moral diagnosis. שְׁרִירוּת לִבָּם (sherirul libam, stubbornness of their heart): this phrase recurs throughout Jeremiah (Jeremiah 3:17; Jeremiah 7:24; Jeremiah 9:13; Jeremiah 11:8; Jeremiah 23:17) — the structural intractability that makes the new covenant (Jeremiah 31) necessary rather than optional. They cling (halakh achar) to other gods.
11: כַּאֲשֶׁר יִדְבַּק הָאֵזוֹר אֶל-מָתְנֵי-אִישׁ (ka'asher yiddaq ha'ezor el-motnei-ish): davaq (cling/cleave) — the same word for the marriage bond (Genesis 2:24), for Israel's commanded devotion to YHWH (Deuteronomy 10:20: uvdo uvo tidbaq, serve him and cling to him), and for Ruth clinging to Naomi (Ruth 1:14). YHWH bound Israel to himself as intimate garment for renown (tiferet), praise (tehillah), and glory (tif'eret) — but they would not listen.
12: Every wineskin (nevel) is filled with wine — a proverb the people use dismissively ('yes, of course!'). But YHWH redirects: I will fill every inhabitant with drunkenness (shikkaron). The wine/judgment equation runs through the prophets (Jeremiah 25:15-17 — the cup of wrath).
13: The drunkenness will cause them to dash (nifatzti) against one another — father against son. YHWH will not spare (lo achmel), pity (lo arachem), or have mercy (lo achus) — triple negation for the completeness of judgment.
14: וְנִפַּצְתִּים אִישׁ אֶל-אָחִיו (venifatztim ish el-achiv, I will dash them man against his brother): the internal violence following covenant-breaking. No pity, no compassion, no mercy — three ways of saying the protective covenant relationship is suspended.
15: שִׁמְעוּ וְהַאֲזִינוּ (shim'u veha'azinu): hear and give ear — synonymous verbs for emphatic summons. אַל-תִּגְבְּהוּ (al-tigbehu, do not be arrogant): the pride-warning before the deportation. כִּי יְהוָה דִּבֵּר (ki YHWH dibber) — the prophetic authority-claim.
16: תְּנוּ לַיהוָה אֱלֹהֵיכֶם כָּבוֹד (tenu laYHWH Eloheykhem kavod, give glory/honor to YHWH your God): kavod (glory/weight) — the call to acknowledge YHWH's weightiness before the darkness of exile falls. Before the feet stumble on the mountains of twilight, before the hoped-for light becomes deep darkness.
17: תִּבְכֶּה נַפְשִׁי בְמִסְתָּרִים (tivkeh nafshi bemistarim, my soul will weep in secret): one of the most intimate prophetic self-revelations — Jeremiah's hidden tears for the flock's captivity. The prophet's inner grief mirrors YHWH's own grief over the covenant people. מִפְּנֵי גַאֲוָה (mipney ga'avah) — because of pride: pride is the reason for exile.
18: The command to king (melekh) and queen mother (gevira): sit low (shiflu), for your crown (ateret tif'artechem, crown of your glory) has fallen. This likely addresses Jehoiachin and Nehushta (2 Kings 24:8) — the royal house goes into exile and its dignity is stripped.
19: The cities of the Negev are shut up (sug), with no one to open them. All Judah is carried into exile — the geography of deportation expanding from south to north. The totality fulfills the covenant curse of Leviticus 26:33.
20: Jerusalem is addressed directly: שְׂאִי עֵינַיִךְ (se'i einayikh, lift up your eyes): see those coming from the north. Where is your beautiful flock that was given to you? The shepherd-imagery turned accusatory — Jerusalem is responsible for the flock she received and failed to protect.
21: What will you say when they appoint your trained allies as head over you? A rhetorical question exposing the bitter irony: the alliances cultivated for security will become Judah's captors. The pain (chavalim) of a woman in childbirth — sudden, unavoidable, consuming.
22: לָמָּה קָרָאנִי זֹאת (lamah qara'ani zo't, why has this happened to me?): Jerusalem's anticipated question. The answer: בְּרֹב עֲוֺנֵךְ (berov avonekh, because of the multitude of your iniquities). The skirt-lifting metaphor: the shame of exposed unfaithfulness as both military defeat and judicial consequence.
23: הֲיַהֲפֹךְ כּוּשִׁי עוֹרוֹ (hayahafokh Kushi oro, can a Cushite change his skin): the Cushite (Kushi) is used for a visually unmistakable difference. וְנָמֵר חֲבַרְבֻּרֹתָיו (venamed chavarburotav, or a leopard its spots): the leopard's pattern is as fixed as the Cushite's skin. Theological point: Judah has become so habituated to evil (hiskhilu laro'a, accustomed to doing evil) that self-generated moral change is as impossible as changing these natural characteristics. One of the OT's most direct statements of the bondage of the will — grounding the need for the new covenant's heart-replacement in Jeremiah 31.
24: כְּקַשׁ עֹבֵר (kekash over, like passing chaff): scattered by the desert wind (ruach midbar). The wind-chaff image for judgment appears in Psalms 1:4 and continues into NT judgment imagery (Matthew 3:12 — chaff burned with unquenchable fire).
25: זֶה גוֹרָלֵךְ מְנָת-מִדַּיִךְ (zeh goralekh menat-midayikh): goral (lot/fate) and menah (measured portion) frame exile as YHWH's judicial apportionment. שָׁכַחְתְּ אוֹתִי (shakacht oti, you have forgotten me): the sin is ultimately relational — forgetting YHWH is the root of all the rest.
26: YHWH himself will lift her skirts over her face — shame of exposed nakedness as both military metaphor for defeat and legal metaphor for the consequences of unfaithfulness. Cf. Ezekiel 16:37; Hosea 2:3.
27: The closing indictment: נִאֻפַיִךְ (ni'ufayikh, your adulteries) — covenant infidelity as sexual unfaithfulness, the Hosea-Jeremiah metaphorical frame; מִצְהֲלוֹתַיִךְ (mitzhalotayikh, your lustful cries/neighings); זִמַּת זְנוּתֵךְ (zimmah znutekh, the obscene acts of your prostitution). The verdict: אוֹי לָךְ יְרוּשָׁלַם (oy lakh Yerushalayim, woe to you, Jerusalem) — covenant lament. Will you not be made clean? How long yet?
